author: @AtlasReburdened
text:
I had an idea a while back for a solid state water pump to be used as an alternative for a hydraulic ram pump and it may be something you could implement using the wind. The schema is fairly simple. A fluid is passed through a honeycomb array or such contrivance to bring it closer to being laminar flow, and this flow is directed onto a parabolic dish which has the open end of a pipe located such that the focus of the parabolic dish is just inside of the mouth of the pipe. The nominal result being that the open mouth of the pipe sees a continuous higher pressure than anywhere else in the device and thusly "pumps" the working fluid into the pipe.
Do you think this schema has any merit?

author: @AtlasReburdened
text:
I'm not sure who told you off about the engine/motor thing.
I've always held to the convention that a motor is strictly a device that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy, itself being a subtype of engine, which is any assembly of moving parts which converts power into mechanical energy.

author: @kb3svj
text:
Hi Robert: 

So, you need an air source huh! 

I did not want to post links in the comment section because I understand that such posting is frowned upon and rude. So, search the following "Harness Hydro Power with a Trompe ".  Search also for an article by the American Society of Mining and Reclamation-(ASMR)  entitled "Trompe design, construction, and performance" - circa 2016.  There you and your readers can discover how to construct a device by which falling water can provide compressed air.  I am not far from two of the areas where the  ASMR featured two Trompes; I may have to go check them out.

Also, a YouTube producer has a video on how he used water from a spigot, though an air garden hose to compress air. For me, since I have well water, I would have to come up with a way of turning that water back into the well after the water-filled vessels are filled. the  YouTube channel describing this means of compressing air is "Quint BUILDs"   - John

author: @williamarmstrong7199
text:
Good old fashioned steam engine technology. I am not sure putting more mass on the piston would help get it over TDC quite the opposite I would think. The place to put the magnets is of course on the flywheel. A Boxer configuration with 2 pistons working on opposition to each other will help balance the harmonics out. Flywheel containing magnets in the middle and a centrifugal clutch allowing the flywheel to freewheel when electrical demand is low will help efficiency and reduce the pumping losses and amount of air needed. This would work well on a wave generator using a column of trapped air. The efficency of these systems is low because the normal difference in water hight is only usually 20 to 40 cm. Trying to get usable power out of such a system means huge systems (and costs) with resultant high maintenence and fragility to big storms. A series of vertical tubes set below the lowest spring tide level connected to small air motors / engines driving a generator which is kept out of the water would help reliability a lot. 
The only issue might be the enviable  build up of  plants and creatures inside the vertical tubes, which are open to the water close to the bottom. Perhaps a floating piston sitting on the top of the water column might act as a scraper and remove most of the critters looking for  somewhere to stick to and live.
Will Teflon stop them?  Using anti-fouling paints such as used on boat hulls is an environmentally bad idea of course.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @NorthernKitty
text: A thought just occurred to me.  If we are eventually making the piston a magnet moving through a coil that is also the cylinder, maybe we could do away with the piston rod entirely?  Have a piston with no rod at all that can slide freely back and forth inside.  To operate the valve,  have a "see-saw" above the cylinder with opposite polarity magnets that are drawn to the piston whenever the piston reaches each end.  When the see-saw is pulled down on one end it opens the valve one direction, and when pulled down at the other end it opens the valve the opposite way.  This would eliminate any potential for air loss.  It would lose the potential for a "steampunk" look, but... I can always make up stuff on the outside for style point anyway.  😋

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are talking about a free piston design and that is where we are heading lol

replies.author: @MrTubeuser12
replies.text: @Tumbleweed-vh4pt what a lot of people don't realize is that Tesla was an amazing mechanical engineer as well, most people just think about his contributions to energy science

replies.author: @Tumbleweed-vh4pt
replies.text: joe bloggs right, it's a crime how Tesla was shunned by the elite and government and not even taught in schools. Very few people are aware of the massive amount of Teslas innovation that is used every day to make our definition of living to this day. I had discovered Tesla thanks to the instructor who taught the electrical course in the trade school I attended in the early 90's. He presented a book that was published in 1981 titled Tesla man out of time by Margaret Cheney. I was the only one in the class that expressed any interest, I guess thanks to the inner nerd in me, and I went and bought a copy, and I was astonished that this man was withheld from educational curriculum. He was well qualified to be in a science, history and mathematics chapter without a doubt. I am glad that people are becoming aware of Tesla and his accomplishments, it sure hasn't been with the assistance of the government and the department of education, it's been more grassroots enlightenment. I can't tell you how many people I have been the first to inform them about Tesla and that he is one of the greatest inventors of technology that we never heard of. I recommend that biography about Tesla it's in paperback and not a huge book to read, but it covers a lot of Teslas life and work. It's a great book to turn onto people who are interested in learning about Tesla in a hurry. The only story that wasn't in it was the one about Teslas electric packard that ran off of atmospheric energy.

author: @NorthernKitty
text: All this time we've been building an "Earthquake Machine?"  Time to break out the zip ties and batten everything down on my workbench!!  😝   Of the 3 valves you've shown us so far, this looks like it might be the most practical for me to 3D print, and I like the linear operation direct with the piston.  I'll be excited to see this working in conjuction with Prepper Tech EE's "water battery". 😊

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - and yes it is a good one to 3d print and I though of you when I made it

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering Oh, no!!  I've got to quit grumbling about "keeping up"... I hope that didn't take any extra time?  I promise to try and make it worth your effort by designing a "steampunk worthy" look to it.  I actually made the cylinder a diameter that a 42mm Munsen ring can hold for that reason.  😊  I still haven't found a source I can order Munsen rings from state-side, but figure I'll just order a bunch at once from the U.K. to hopefully keep shipping costs down.  That, or I'll just 3D design and print what I need with a metal-looking filament.  Maybe I should just print it, anyway, if I'm going to post the build to Thingiverse for others?  Ultimately, when I've completed this project and happy with the designs, I want to print the cylinder with a wood filament, sand it down and stain it.  Maybe throw in a few LED's in vacuum tubes, some gauges and a few gratuitous turning gears.  Maybe a heating element to burn drops of oil to emit smoke.  😋   But please don't hold me to it, I have a tendency to dream big and fail hard!!  (But at least I get to keep the dreams.)  It will truly be a miracle enough if I complete a working engine!

author: @darrellpidgeon6440
text: I love anything Tesla.  I still own a copy of a 1968 publication called Science Fair Projects, and in it's pages are instructions for building a Tesla coil.  It uses vacuum tubes.  As a thirteen year old interested in science, the thing was definitely intriguing.  Of course it was next to impossible to find much material on Tesla himself, back then.  Also in that publication was how to build a mini Van deGraff generator (did I spell that right?).  Today I find the internet a valuable source of information.  So glad I found your channel to help feed my restless imagination.

replies.text: I am really glad you like the channel mate

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
This is the rocking valve I was describing on one of the earlier remarks I made before you got beyond the flat sliding valve body.

What would be interesting is making a wind turbine or ocean wave driven air compressor. 
~( 'w')/

While the pressure would be terribly low on the first stage, one can use wave rise and fall as a liquid air compressor [results vary depending on such issues as how high the surf and the tide are, of course] or one could use just a float on a lever to drive a piston [of any given size] for each stage from the highest volume, lowest pressure first stage, to the lowest volume, highest pressure last stage [with as many (or as few) stages in between as one felt like messing with to get their intended volume and pressure of air, and at the same time, the sea water can be used to dissipate the heat of compressing the air to keep the whole thing cool enough to not overheat since it might be made all out of plastic.

Granted, since this is already a very low pressure rig, I would not get too bothered making the compressor too overly complicated, and would rather focus on getting enough volume on hand to keep the motor happy.

text: I will never forget the day I started shaking the brick building I lived in.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @XUNIXMUSIC
replies.text: with the tesla device, if yes from which Site/guide did you build it?

replies.author: @MAGnetICus_Attractus
replies.text: @XUNIXMUSIC I made a couple of videos  recently.  I made the magnet version not a air one like this video. I was honestly surprised 2 or 3 microwave magnets could lift a 5 pound weight. Then a coil from  a bathroom exhaust fan motor to drive the 5 pound piston.  Trust me that 5 pound piston will bottom out on the floor and things start shaking. I know Tesla didn't have all the microcrud devices back then so I tend to tinker on a primitive level. I really can't remember what book I read at the public library that had drawings of things Nikola Tesla devised.  Try Google or whatever search engine for "Tesla Magnetic Oscillators". They were very popular with the ladies and many needed a toilet when it would shake their bowels loose according to the book. Tesla pancake coils work best.

author: @michaelinamoment
text: I've always wondered if Tesla's Earthquake machine was the forefather of the Tuned Mass Dampener used in skyscrapers today.

replies.author: @lostinthemoonlight
replies.text: Yes, they definitely were influenced by Tesla’s ideas. What wasn’t?

replies.author: @michaelinamoment
replies.text: @lostinthemoonlight I assumed it did but I don't know if it's just one of those ideas that Tesla never got credit for.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it probably was mate - but there have been independent examples around the world without Tesla - in Japan for example they have used mass damping since the 15th or 16th century

replies.author: @michaelinamoment
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering oh that's interesting! If the idea of predates Tesla all Tesla had to do was take the principles and run it in the reverse for an earthquake machine and a skyscraper! It's funny how often people dismiss Tesla because of the mystique around him but it seems like it's pretty sound principles that you can use resonance to topple a tall structure.
